1: The word-event formula and the command to set the prophetic face toward the mountains of Israel. The mountains as recipients of judgment oracle anticipate the day when Christ the true mountain of Israel will replace the high places with himself — the new mountain to which all nations will stream (Isaiah 2:2-3; Matthew 5:1).
2: 'Son of man, set your face against the mountains of Israel and prophesy against them' — the prophetic confrontation with Israel's illegitimate worship sites. Jesus similarly confronts the established religious sites of his day, overturning the temple tables (John 2:13-17) and predicting the temple's destruction (Matthew 24:2). The prophet's face set against the mountains of corruption is the type of the Messiah's face set steadfastly toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51).
3: 'Your altars will be desolated and your incense altars will be shattered, and I will make your slain fall before your idols' — the destruction of the false worship sites. The desolation of the high places and their altars points to the new worship that Christ introduces: a time when the Father will be worshiped neither on Mount Gerizim nor Jerusalem but in spirit and truth (John 4:21-24). The obliteration of false altars makes way for the one altar that counts — the cross of Christ.
4: The slain thrown before their idols — the irony of idol-worship: those who bowed to images become corpses before them. Paul argues that idol-worship is ultimately self-destructive (1 Corinthians 10:19-22); what is sacrificed to idols goes to demons, and those who commune with demons share their fate. Christ's triumph over the principalities and powers (Colossians 2:15) is the eschatological form of this high-place destruction.
5: 'I will lay the corpses of the Israelites before their idols and scatter your bones around your altars' — the defilement of the altars by the bones of the worshipers is deeply ironic. In Christ, the relationship between body and altar is reversed: he gives his body at the altar of the cross, not as a victim of meaningless death but as the willing sacrifice whose blood purifies the altar (Hebrews 13:10-12).
6: The comprehensive desolation of the high places and their cult infrastructure. The end of Israel's self-organized worship makes way for the worship that God himself establishes through Christ. The desolate high places of judgment anticipate the eschatological truth that 'the LORD alone will be exalted in that day' (Isaiah 2:17).
7: 'The slain will fall in your midst, and you will know that I am the LORD' — recognition through judgment. The pattern recurs throughout Ezekiel: judgment produces the knowledge of YHWH that devotion to idols prevented. The cross of Christ is the ultimate instance of this pattern: the judgment that fell on the Son produces the knowledge of the Father's love that all other religion failed to deliver (Romans 5:8).
8: 'But I will spare some of you — some will escape the sword among the nations when you are scattered through the countries' — the remnant promise within the judgment oracle. Paul identifies the eschatological remnant as those chosen by grace: 'So too, at the present time there is a remnant chosen by grace' (Romans 11:5). The survivors scattered among the nations are the type of the Jewish remnant who believe in Jesus and the Gentile believers grafted in through the same grace.
9: 'Then your survivors will remember me among the nations to which they have been exiled — how I was broken by their adulterous hearts' — YHWH's broken heart at Israel's idolatry. The language of divine heartbreak prefigures the NT's insistence that sin is relational betrayal, not merely legal violation. Jesus weeps over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44) because the infidelity that broke YHWH's heart in Ezekiel's day culminates in the generation that rejects the Son.
10: 'They will know that I am the LORD; I did not threaten in vain to bring this disaster on them' — YHWH vindicating his word through fulfillment. Jesus uses the same self-vindicating logic: 'I am telling you now before it happens, so that when it does happen you will believe that I am he' (John 13:19). Ezekiel's prophetic authority established through fulfilled judgment anticipates Christ's authority established through his announced death and resurrection.
11: 'Strike your hands together and stamp your foot and cry out in grief over all the evil and detestable practices of the house of Israel' — the embodied grief of the prophet over Israel's sin. Jesus similarly embodies grief over Israel's unbelief (John 11:33-35; Luke 19:41). The prophet who grieves over what he must announce points to the Messiah who weeps over the judgment he must pronounce and bear.
12: 'The one far away will die by plague; the one nearby will fall by the sword; and the one who survives and is spared will die by famine. So I will exhaust my wrath upon them' — the comprehensive judgment from which there is no escape by geography. Only the cross provides a way through YHWH's exhausted wrath: Christ became the one on whom YHWH's wrath was fully spent (Romans 3:25; 1 John 2:2), opening the way for the remnant to survive.
13: 'You will know that I am the LORD when their slain lie among their idols around their altars, on every high hill and mountain top, under every leafy tree and every thick oak' — the green tree motif of illicit worship (cf. Deuteronomy 12:2; Jeremiah 2:20; Ezekiel 3:6). Jesus as the new tree planted by YHWH (Isaiah 11:1; John 15:1-5) replaces every false tree under which Israel had worshiped. The branch of Jesse becomes the tree of life, replacing the trees of idolatry with the one tree that gives life.
14: 'I will stretch out my hand against them and make the land desolate and waste everywhere they live, from the wilderness to Riblah' — the outstretched hand of divine judgment from south (the Negev wilderness) to north (Riblah near Hamath). The geographic comprehensiveness of the judgment is reversed in the geographic comprehensiveness of the gospel: from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8). The same hand outstretched in judgment is stretched out in invitation through the gospel (Isaiah 65:2; Romans 10:21).
